data.title: REG - HSBC Holdings PLC - Transaction in Own Shares
data.description:
RNS Number : 4860L HSBC Holdings PLC 04 June 2025  

HSBC HOLDINGS PLC

4 June 2025

Transaction in own shares

HSBC Holdings plc ("HSBC" or the "Company") announces that it has purchased for cancellation the following number of its ordinary shares of US$0.50 from Morgan Stanley & Co. International plc ("Morgan Stanley") as part of its buy-back announced on 6 May 2025.

UK Venues

Date of purchase:

4 June 2025

Number of ordinary shares of US$0.50 each purchased:

1,452,321

Highest price paid per share:

£8.8000 

Lowest price paid per share:

£8.7050 

Volume weighted average price paid per share:

£8.7378 

All repurchases on the London Stock Exchange, Aquis Exchange, Cboe Europe Limited (through the BXE and CXE order books) and/or Turquoise ("UK Venues") are implemented as "on Exchange" transactions (as such term is defined in the rules of the London Stock Exchange) and as "market purchases" for the purposes of the Companies Act 2006.

Hong Kong Stock Exchange

Date of purchase:

4 June 2025

Number of ordinary shares of US$0.50 each purchased:

1,029,600

Highest price paid per share:

HK$93.0500

Lowest price paid per share:

HK$92.4000

Volume weighted average price paid per share:

HK$92.7645

All repurchases on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited ("Hong Kong Stock Exchange") are "off market" for the purposes of the Companies Act 2006 but are transactions which occur "on Exchange" for the purposes of the Rules Governing the Listing of Securities on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited and which constitute an "on-market share buy-back" for the purposes of the Codes on Takeovers and Mergers and Share Buy-backs.

Since the commencement of the buy-back announced on 6 May 2025, the Company has repurchased 132,191,751 ordinary shares for a total consideration of approximately US$1,537.3m.

Following the cancellation of the shares repurchased on the UK Venues, the Company's issued ordinary share capital will consist of 17,559,642,786 ordinary shares with voting rights. There are no ordinary shares held in treasury. Cancellation of the shares repurchased on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange takes longer than those repurchased on the UK Venues and a further announcement of total voting rights will be made once those shares have been cancelled.

The above figure of 17,559,642,786 may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the Financial Conduct Authority's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

In accordance with Article 5(1)(b) of the Market Abuse Regulation (EU) No 596/2014 (as it forms part of domestic law of the United Kingdom by virtue of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, as amended), a full breakdown of the individual trades made by Morgan Stanley on behalf of the Company is available via the link below.

http://www.rns-pdf.londonstockexchange.com/rns/4860L_1-2025-6-4.pdf

This announcement will also be available on HSBC's website at www.hsbc.com/sea

data.title: Russia Jobless Rate Steady at Record Low of 2.3%
data.description:
The unemployment rate in Russia stood at a record low of 2.3% in April 2025, unchanged from March, down slightly from the forecast of 2.4%, as a result of an acute labour shortage caused by the war in Ukraine.

The number of unemployed individuals fell to 1.709 million from 1.730 million in the month before.

Meanwhile, the number of officially registered unemployed ticked down to 0.336 million from 0.337 million.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1035 ET - EU merger enforcers should let European telecoms companies consolidate to enable them to compete globally, Justin Hotard, president and CEO of Finnish company Nokia, says at a conference in Brussels. "If we just simply allow European telecom operators to consolidate--in many countries, from four to three--like you've seen in the U.S., like you've seen in India. If we allow that to happen, what we'll see is greater scale," he says. "We'll unlock true investment in innovation and 5G and 6G," he says. Consolidation could also lead to faster investments in broadband and mobile, he says. "You have to allow a regulatory environment that permits scale." (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

1035 ET - CrowdStrike's valuation leaves only limited upside from its current level, BofA Securities analysts say in a research note. The cybersecurity company swung to a loss in 1Q, and its revenue guidance for the 2Q came in below the Street's expectations. The analysts are positive on the healthy underlying trends in 1Q, with growth in annual recurring revenue and products outside the core offering improving. But its 2Q guidance is a negative, particularly as the analysts see growth decelerating on an annual basis looking out to 2027. The analysts downgrade CrowdStrike to neutral from buy but raise their price objective to $470 from $420. Shares decline 4.3% to $467.50. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0955 ET - J.P. Morgan analysts say in a research note that Hewlett Packard Enterprise delivered a positive surprise in the recent quarter, posting solid results and guidance. The server and cloud-software company highlighted better-than-expected artificial-intelligence server revenue in its F2Q. The company remains on track for a strong current quarter thanks to revenue realization from a large cluster deployment, as well as its selective deals strategy, the analysts say. At the same time, there is little evidence of slowdowns in HPE's non-AI segments. The recent report allows HPE sidestep "the high-competitive intensity around AI servers, as well as broader macro concerns that had been building up into the print," the analysts write.(connor.hart@wsj.com)

0945 ET - Raymond James says, "Hewlett Packard Enterprise has prioritized profits, particularly in the artificial-intelligence market," and this contrasts with its competitors. "We consider this a smart strategy that can play into its strength with enterprises and high-performance computing," the analysts write. The company continues to work through challenges in the business that hurt earnings in the recent quarter, the analysts note. Still, the company appears to be on the right track, and the challenges didn't hold the firm back from posting a beat-and-raise quarter. Hewlett Packard Enterprise rises 3% in early trading. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0603 ET - Germany's DAX rises to a new high in morning trade after the German cabinet approved Chancellor Friedrich Merz's tax plan to stimulate Europe's biggest economy. The German blue-chip index is up 1% at 24337.20 after rising as high as 24346.15, a new record. The German cabinet approved Wednesday a proposal to introduce corporate tax breaks and support the country's economy. Infineon Technologies, Airbus and DHL owner Deutsche Post top the DAX's gainers. The DAX last closed at a record level on May 27, when it ended the session at 24226.49. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0455 ET - S4 Capital's disclosure of T-Mobile as the name behind a previously announced technology client win is the most significant news in an update ahead of its annual general meeting, Panmure Liberum's Johnathan Barrett and Sean Kealy say. The U.K. digital advertising firm cut its full-year revenue expectations to a low-single-percentage-digit decline. However, Panmure Liberum had already forecast a 2% fall out of caution about the economic environment. "The only real new news is the clarification of T-Mobile as being the mystery client behind the significant technology win previously announced," the analysts say in a note. Shares rise 5.6% to 27.20 pence. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0141 ET - Prosus shares are likely to fall on Chief Investment Officer Ervin Tu's departure given his track record and strong understanding of capital markets, Citi writes. However, the bank says Tu's departure shouldn't be a surprise given the company's focus on its e-commerce portfolio, from a holding company. Prosus said Tu was stepping down from his role as CIO and group president but will remain an advisor to the group. Shares closed at 44.885 euros and are up 17% over the year to date. (ian.walker@wsj.com.)

0033 ET - JOYY's near-term outlook may be supported by the advertising business and strong shareholder returns, says Deutsche Bank Research analyst Leo Chiang in a research note. Non-live-streaming revenue growth is likely to accelerate in 2Q, particularly in terms of ads, driven by favorable seasonality and the company's increasing emphasis on this business, Chiang says. Still, the analyst lowers the FY 2025 revenue forecast by 3% on a weaker live-streaming outlook, but keeps the adjusted net profit estimate largely unchanged to reflect a better margin outlook. The investment bank says the company's strong shareholder returns are attractive. It maintains a buy rating with a target price of $60.00. The ADRs last traded at $7.03. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

1836 ET - CrowdStrike has an opportunity to expand its market thanks to agentic artificial intelligence because AI creates a bigger risk of cybersecurity breaches, the company said during an analyst call Tuesday. AI Agents have access to multiple internal and external data stores, applications, and business processes. They increase the size and severity of and speed at which an attack can happen. "CrowdStrike is uniquely positioned to secure the identity, the workload, the infrastructure, the data and underlying AI models themselves," Chief Executive George Kurtz said. "CrowdStrike will be the protector of autonomous agents." (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1829 ET - Guidewire Software saw strong results in both deal counts and sizes in its latest quarter, CFO Jeff Cooper says during the company's earnings call. Insurers appear more willing to make large commitments now that the company has established its position in the cloud market, he says. Maintaining momentum with large, multi-year deals in the company's key 4Q will be key to determining the company's outlook, he says. The company hopes to notch a third year of high-teens fully-ramped ARR growth for a third year in a row, he says.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1818 ET - Guidewire Software saw growing demand across each tier of the property and casualty insurance industry in its latest quarter, with particular strength from the high-end of the market, CEO Mike Rosenbaum says during the company's earnings call. The company closed 17 cloud deals in the quarter, with seven core system deals with Tier 1 insurers and three with Tier 2 insurers, he says. Insurers are continuing to look to replace rigid systems with more agile platforms that allow them to deploy new products and lines of business faster, he says. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1748 ET - HealthEquity executives are praising the proposed expansion to health savings accounts that were included in the tax-and-spending bill passed by the House of Representatives last month. They were in Washington D.C. last month to advocate for just such expansions, CEO Scott Cutler says on a call with analysts. "It was serendipity that the House released their draft of the budget bill while we were there," he says. The provisions would give up to 20 million more American families access to the benefits of HSAs, founder Steve Neeleman says on the call. The provisions still have a ways to go before being implemented, including approval from the Senate. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1046 ET - Natural disasters and migration patterns loom large over state budgets in the U.S. "We are seeing more disasters and we are seeing more expensive disasters," Conning's Karel Citroen says. "In part, that is because people have moved to areas that are more more exposed" such as Florida and Texas, he says. As a result, insurance costs have skyrocketed in these locations, increasing living costs and pushing people to move out, eroding the local tax base. In a recent study, Conning found that international immigration boosted population growth in Florida, Texas and Nevada, while Utah and South Carolina remained popular destinations for retirees and people fleeing higher costs elsewhere. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1005 ET - The future of European banking hinges on frictionless digital experiences and a shift towards an investment-savvy culture, ING Group's CEO says at the Money20/20 conference in Amsterdam. Steven van Rijswijk notes that there is strong mortgage demand in Europe fueled by economic growth and low unemployment. He also highlights the need for banks to empower everyday individuals to invest safely, even in small amounts. "It if we want to stimulate the economy, we also need to make sure that people invest, invest in companies, invest in SMEs," he says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0951 ET - UniCredit's exit from its Russian subsidiary would represent a capital hit of around 24 basis points, Jefferies says in a note after the Italian press reported terms for a potential sale of the unit to UAE-based funds at a $1.2 billion valuation. The estimate calculated by analysts compares with a cost of an "extreme loss" scenario in Russia of around 80 basis points, as per disclosure by the Italian bank. "Despite the low asset valuation, we believe that an exit would remove an overhang on the stock and de-risk the bank's excess capital, which benefited from the Ruble revaluation in the last quarter," analysts write. UniCredit's recently increased stake in Greek lender Alpha Bank could fill the income gap left by a potential Russia exit, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0937 ET - CaixaBank's CEO views new fintechs and digital banks as an incentive for traditional banks to innovate, rather than a direct threat to their position. Competition from these agile players has prompted incumbents to develop efficient systems, Gonzalo Gortazar says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. He cites Spain's popular payments solution Bizum. "We have to be able to incorporate everything that the new players bring us, who are highly innovative, and at the same time combine it with our solidity and with our people," he says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0934 ET - Large-scale cross-border retail banking mergers in Europe are generally unprofitable and complex, CaixaBank's CEO says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. Despite having a central supervisory authority, varied tax, consumer protection, and commercial regulations create significant friction. This is compounded by differing insolvency regimes and structurally dissimilar national systems, Gonzalo Gortazar says. CaixaBank's own acquisition of BPI in Portugal generated value due to the regional convergence, he says. However, the broader scene for large deals across borders is quite blurry, he adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0911 ET - Swedbank's investor day didn't contain any big surprises and the overall message is reassuring, JPMorgan analysts write. The bank's targets are broadly in line with previous guidance and somewhat expected, the analysts add. Swedbank aims to maintain a return on equity of at least 15% by 2027 and a cost/income ratio of 40% or below. "We expect limited EPS changes with consensus already forecasting 39% cost/income." JPMorgan says focus remains on the usage of excess capital, with potential upside to consensus dividend expectations. However, ongoing probes into anti-money laundering shortcomings remain an overhang and more visibility could be required to discount more payout. Shares fall 0.6%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0802 ET - The decade-long inertia gripping Europe's banking and capital-markets union projects is detrimental to citizens and inhibits economic growth, CaixaBank's CEO says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. The stagnation of the deposit guarantee fund is due to outdated fears about southern European banks, despite the view among investors that these are highly attractive markets, Gonzalo Gortazar says. There is an enormous reluctance among regulators and governments to cede small parcels of sovereignty, which is preventing a true single market for capital and investment, he notes. This leads Europe to watch its savings flow abroad to more efficient markets like the U.S., undermining domestic investment opportunities and competitiveness. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0724 ET - Crypto markets have suffered headwinds from cautious market sentiment, says LMAX Group's Joel Kruger in a note. U.S. listed spot bitcoin ETFs record a third day of net outflows. Economic pressures, U.S.-China trade tensions and looming tariff hikes contribute to market uncertainty, removing some of the positives for crypto right now, he says. However, ETF outflows were somewhat offset by an accumulation of holdings by so-called bitcoin 'whales', which are individuals or organizations with large bitcoin holdings, Kruger says. Bitcoin and ethereum are therefore likely to remain range-bound. Investors await upcoming U.S. data for clues on future Federal Reserve policy and the impact of tariffs, he says. Bitcoin falls 0.2% to $105,628. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0719 ET - ING's operations in Spain benefit from being part of an international bank with a market cap nearing 60 billion euros while operating as a fintech in the country, the CEO for the Dutch bank's Spanish and Portuguese markets says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. ING entered Spain in 1999 as a telephone bank and is now almost fully digital, operating with only 29 branches nationwide. Alfonso Tolcheff explains that the group chose Madrid to establish a new technology hub and plans to create one thousand STEM jobs in the next four years, reinforcing its commitment to the region. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0631 ET - Unicaja's president says he found a strong impetus for change and moving forward after the bank's complex merger with Liberbank when he joined the company almost a year ago. The bank recently outlined a strategic plan for the three years to 2027, which aims to create a better entity that is more client-focused and has a simpler management structure. "We have many things to do and I always think that is positive, because if we didn't have anything to do...there wouldn't be much additional value contribution," Jose Sevilla says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. Unicaja and Liberbank merged three years ago. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0618 ET - Being a part of the landscape and having deep local knowledge is key for smaller banks to be competitive, Ibercaja's president says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. This generates a web of connections, making the bank a determining actor when it comes to fostering economic, social and cultural development, Francisco Jose Serrano says. The bank has been around for a century and a half and has always aimed to place the client at the center of its operations, he says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0609 ET - Internal simplification has been the cornerstone of Unicaja's strategy to foster client "closeness", the Spanish bank's president Jose Sevilla says at the IESE Banking conference in Madrid. Proximity banking goes beyond physical presence to encompass digital accessibility and consumer choice, he says. Sevilla adds that the lender has focused on creating agility in processes and has streamlined its product catalog to 200 products from 2000 a year ago. This internal focus, which also extends to the simplification of the organizational structure, is a key comparative advantage against larger entities, he notes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1152 ET - Brookfield Renewable Partners should still be able to generate good returns for investors despite losing support from the U.S. Raymond James' Frederic Bastien says, as drafted, Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act proposes to eliminate the clean electricity investment tax credits for facilities that don't begin construction within 60 days after the Bill's enactment, or that aren't operational by the end of 2028. But Bastien isn't worried for Brookfield. "This does not concern us one bit since as a large, technology agnostic, and globally diversified renewable operator and developer, BEP is not beholden to any one government subsidy," Bastien says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0910 ET - Oil futures slip into the red following two days of gains. Prospects of higher OPEC+ are offset in the near term by lower Canadian production due to wildfires and geopolitical tensions. "Our short-term view of WTI remains one in which trade is likely to confine further to about the $60 to $64 zone," Ritterbusch says in a note. A test of last month's futures high of $64.19 "cannot be ruled out if the dollar weakens further and the up-trend in the equities continues," the firm adds. WTI and Brent are down 0.6% at $63.04 and $65.26 a barrel, respectively.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0458 ET - Repsol isn't in a hurry to list its upstream business, RBC Capital Markets analysts write in a note after speaking with the Spanish energy major's finance chief Antonio Lorenzo. Repsol is committed to preparing the business for an initial public offering in 2026 but any transaction will depend on market conditions, they write. Repsol appears to be considering all options. This includes merging with a public or private U.S. exploration and production company, they add. A key criteria for Repsol is maintaining control of the new entity and the internal focus is on governance structures within the division, they say. Shares trade up 0.4% at 12.20 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0219 ET - Seatrium appears to have secured a strong start to its financial year, UOB Kay Hian's Adrian Loh says in a research report. Its 1Q business update shows successful delivery of ExxonMobil's One Guyana floating production, storage, and offloading unit and completion of 45 repair and upgrade projects, the analyst notes. Also, the integrated shipyard recently won a floating storage regasification unit conversion contract from Norway's Höegh Evi, which will commence late 1H. These projects signal strong order intake and underpin Seatrium's multi-year revenue visibility up to 2031. The brokerage has a buy rating and target price of S$2.96 on the stock, which is up 1.00% at S$2.05. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1511 ET - Oil futures post back-to-back gains on a series of supply risks that offset concerns about OPEC+ plans for higher output. "Multiple factors have conspired to push the barrel higher," Mizuho's Robert Yawger says in a note. They include reports that Canadian wildfires have shut in more than 300,000 barrels a day in production, doubts about the U.S. and Iran reaching a nuclear deal, and the possibility of tighter sanctions on Russia. On the downside is the OECD cutting its global and U.S. economic growth forecasts, and weak manufacturing data out of China, Yawger adds. WTI settles up 1.4% at $63.41 a barrel and Brent rises 1.5% to $65.63. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1025 ET - Canada's largest public sector labor union is calling on the federal government to counter President Trump's increased tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum and introduce investment protections. "These tariffs are killing investment in our steel, aluminum, and auto sectors, and we are already seeing the consequences in lost jobs and economic instability," Unifor National President Lana Payne says. The union wants retaliatory tariffs on U.S. steel and aluminum to match the 50% rate and new border measures to prevent foreign steel and aluminum from being dumped in Canada. It also wants a temporary halt to exports of strategic metals to the U.S. and the building of a stockpile reserve. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0858 ET - Canada arguably doesn't need to retaliate against the doubling of the U.S. tariff on imports of aluminum and steel to 50%, since America is doing enough damage to itself as it fights all its major trading partners, Scotiabank's Derek Holt suggests. The economist notes the average effective tariff rate on U.S. imports increases with this move to 12% on all goods and services, or 14.6% on just goods. "That's a severe terms of trade hit to the U.S. economy compared to around a 2% effective rate on all imports before all of this nonsense began." Prime Minister Mark Carney has indicated a preference for "intensive and live negotiations," though Holt says that hopefully doesn't include some "Golden Dome" fiasco at the expense of Canadian taxpayers. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0350 ET - Mining stocks lead the gains on the FTSE 100 index in early morning trade. The rise reverses Tuesday's fall when they were among the largest fallers after the OECD downgraded its forecast for global economic growth. U.K. investors look eager to shrug off U.S. trade drama, Hargreaves Lansdown's Matt Britzman writes. Meanwhile, gold and base metal prices ticked up in early trade, providing a lift to the stocks. Silver and gold miner Fresnillo traded up 2.6% while Chilean copper miner Antofagasta rose 2.5%. Anglo American and Glencore were both up around 1.9% while Endeavour Mining was up 1.5%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0250 ET - Aarti Industries' performance is likely to improve meaningfully, Axis Securities analysts say in a note. This performance is underpinned by the specialty chemicals manufacturer's strategic focus on operational efficiency and cost optimization, the analysts say. The Indian company is also intensifying its focus on developing new products, especially within the ethylation value chain, the analysts say. This is supported by Aarti Industries' recent production capacity expansions aimed at improving asset utilization and broadening its product portfolio. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of INR520.00 on the stock, which is up 0.7% at INR473.80. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0109 ET - There are several ways Glencore shareholders might benefit if the miner pursues a merger with Rio Tinto, say Citi analysts Ephrem Ravi and Paul McTaggart. Glencore would gain exposure to iron-ore earnings, and growth from copper and lithium, they say. That would give it a much bigger pool of marketing options. If Glencore carves off coal assets to marry its metals operations with Rio Tinto's, that metals business could enjoy a rerating, say the analysts. Shareholders would retain exposure separately to the lucrative energy assets. Australian investors could also get fully franked dividends. For Rio Tinto, the main benefit of a tie-up would be diversification away from iron ore, the analysts say. The steel ingredient today accounts for most of Rio Tinto's earnings. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2149 ET - Affin Hwang IB analysts see Malaysia's equities market remaining volatile in 3Q amid global uncertainties, including the end of the 90-day pause on U.S. reciprocal tariffs and ongoing geopolitical tensions. However, they remain positive on Malaysia's strong investment cycle, structural reforms, and data centre expansion. Affin Hwang keeps its overweight call on Malaysia's equities market and retains its end-2025 KLCI target at 1650. It replaces Maybank with CIMB Group as a top market buy on more attractive valuations, and adds Petronas Chemicals and Sime Darby to its sell list, citing their disappointing 1Q earnings. The benchmark KLCI is down 0.1% at 1502.36. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1658 ET - The Federal Reserve is holding down demand for steel used in construction and manufacturing by not cutting interest rates, Cleveland-Cliffs CEO Lourenco Goncalves says. "The Fed is ridiculously late in acting," Goncalves says during remarks to reporters at a meeting of the American Iron and Steel Institute, a steel industry trade group. "For the steel industry, it's killing steel consumption." (robert.tita@wsj.com)

1418 ET - Gold futures backtrack after rising the previous day. The front-month contract falls 0.6% to $3,350.20 a troy ounce. The reversal comes after the U.S. dollar rises 0.5%. Analysts say gold prices are likely to mixed in coming days. "Gold's near-term outlook remains volatile; future movements will mostly depend on the evolution of US trade policy and signals from the Fed on their path regarding monetary policy," says ADM Investor Services in a note. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1615 GMT - Rev Group heads for the exits for some of its product lines. The maker of specialty vehicles and related aftermarket parts says it will exit the non-motorized travel trailer and truck camper product categories through a sale of the Lance Camper business, and discussions with prospective buyers have begun. "As our only non-motorized business over time, it has become a less significant part of our overall strategic focus with scale that no longer aligns with the broader direction of our portfolio," CEO Mark Skonieczny says on an earnings call. He notes challenged from the geographic distance between this business located in Southern California and the core RV business in Indiana. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1608 GMT - Rev Group saw minimal impact from tariffs in 2Q thanks to around three months of inventory as it entered the quarter. The maker of specialty vehicles and related aftermarket parts expects a $5 million impact within the RV segment in the second half of the year related to luxury van chassis imported from Europe. CEO Mark Skonieczny on an earnings call says that impact is related to purchases and commitments that were already in place, and the company has transitioned future purchases of the chassis to domestic plants. Rev's adjusted guidance includes a roughly $10 million second half impact of tariffs on its material spend largely within its specialty vehicles segment. Shares surge 15% to $42.78, on pace for a new all-time high. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1325 GMT - Thor Industries expects a tough next two quarters. "We expect the fourth quarter of our fiscal 2025 and the first quarter of our fiscal 2026 to be challenging," says CEO Bob Martin. "The current economic uncertainty has led to downward pressure on consumer confidence and has negatively impacted retail pull-through." The maker of Airstream and Jayco RVs expects things to improve as uncertainty dissipates. The company is maintaining its outlook for the fiscal year, even as it recognizes uncertainty may grow depending on the macroeconomic environment. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1322 GMT - Thor Industries is seeing stark differences in demand for RVs in North America and Europe. The maker of Airstream and Jayco RVs reports sales and shipments for both North American towable RVs and motorized RVs rising from a year earlier, partly due to an increase in price. Sales and shipments for Europeans RVs decline from the prior-year, however. Pressure on margins persists as it manages through softer retail and wholesale demand in the North American motorized and European segments, says COO Todd Woelfer. Thor rises 9% premarket on stronger F3Q results. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1300 GMT - DHL's express unit is its most important division and Citi says the market underestimates the EBIT margin opportunity as volumes recover. The unit recorded an operating margin of 12.3% in 2024 and management has guided for a figure in the mid-teens by 2030. Citi suggests that as volumes recover, the unit can deliver a 13.4% EBIT margin in 2026 and 13.7% in 2027, versus company-compiled consensus of 12.6% and 13.7% respectively. "The express segment has shown resilience in the past few years of volume decline, and we are close to see a potential volume recovery." Citi upgrades the stock to buy from neutral and lifts its target price to 48 euros from 40 euros. Shares rise 1.4% to 40.15 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1003 GMT - Germany's DAX rises to a new high in morning trade after the German cabinet approved Chancellor Friedrich Merz's tax plan to stimulate Europe's biggest economy. The German blue-chip index is up 1% at 24337.20 after rising as high as 24346.15, a new record. The German cabinet approved Wednesday a proposal to introduce corporate tax breaks and support the country's economy. Infineon Technologies, Airbus and DHL owner Deutsche Post top the DAX's gainers. The DAX last closed at a record level on May 27, when it ended the session at 24226.49. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0754 GMT - Tata Motors' newly launched Harrier.ev electric vehicle is likely a competitive offering versus its peers', Nomura analysts say in a note. Overall positioning of the Harrier.ev is similar to Mahindra & Mahindra's attractive feature-loaded battery EVs with long ranges, the analysts note. Such products should help boost EV penetration in India this year. Sales of the Harrier's Internal Combustion Engine model has been around 1,500 units a month over the past few months, and Harrier sales should receive a boost with the EV launch. Nomura has a neutral rating and target price of INR799.00 on the stock, which is up 0.6% at INR707.95. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0646 GMT - NIO may need to raise more funding for the rest of this year, Nomura analysts write in a note. The Chinese automaker keeps its plan to improve gross profit margin as its top priority rather than shipment volume. NIO faces challenges in its financials, Nomura says. The brokerage remains cautious on NIO's 2025 business plan to achieve strong sales growth with new model launches alongside cost-reduction efforts. If NIO can reach monthly sales of 25,000 units for each of its NIO and Onvo brands into 4Q, there could be re-rating opportunities ahead. Nomura maintains its neutral rating and target price at $5.00. Shares last closed at $3.53. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0437 GMT - NIO might not break even before 2027, Bernstein analysts say in a note after the Chinese automaker delivered another quarter of weaker-than-expected results. The company's 1Q gross margin was 7.6%, which Bernstein called disappointing. Its vehicle margin deteriorated to 10.2% from 13.1% in the previous quarter due to operating deleveraging resulting from lower production volume, the analysts add. NIO's management still thinks it can break even in 4Q this year, but Bernstein holds a more conservative view given the intensifying competition in the SUV market. It cuts its ADR target price to $4.00 from $4.50. U.S.-listed NIO last closed at $3.53. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Italian Stocks Steady Amid Tariff Tensions
data.description:
The Italian stock market closed near flat at 40,080 on Wednesday, Milan recovered after spending most of the session in negative territory, held back by bank shares and Leonardo, Piazza Affari stock gains in telecommunications and construction helped offset losses.

Markets remain cautious after the US raised tariffs on steel and aluminum to 50%, while Trump signaled difficulty in reaching a trade deal with China.

Eurozone services PMI slipped to 49.7 in May, indicating contraction, while the composite index eased to 50.2.

Italy outperformed, with its services PMI rising to 53.2.

On the corporate front, STMicroelectronics jumped 11.15% amid AI optimism and reorganization rumors.

Campari (+6.37%) and Saipem (+2.27%) also rose.

On the downside, Mediobanca fell 2.61% and Leonardo dropped 2.46%.

data.title: ZA Miner Expands Global Access to Eco-Driven Crypto Mining as Individual Investors Seek Stability

LONDON, UK, June 04, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — ZA Miner, a cloud mining company operating over 100 renewable-energy-powered facilities worldwide, has seen a sharp rise in user growth in Q2 2025. This increase follows a broader shift among individual investors seeking alternatives to traditional assets, amid early signs of a potential rebound in the digital asset market.

According to company insights, a large portion of new users are entering from mainstream trading environments, attracted to ZA Miner’s simplified approach to cryptocurrency income generation. These users, ranging from first-time crypto participants to seasoned investors, are opting for managed mining contracts as a low-barrier entry point to digital asset exposure.

Automated Cloud Mining Gains Popularity Among Retail Investors Recent user data reflects a growing preference for automated systems that remove the complexity often associated with cryptocurrency mining. ZA Miner’s platform allows users to access blockchain-based income streams without the need for direct trading, hardware management, or energy concerns. Mining operations are powered entirely by renewable sources, including wind and hydropower, supporting the platform’s long-standing commitment to environmental sustainability.

A Scalable Infrastructure With Global Reach

Founded in 2020, ZA Miner operates across North America, Europe, and Asia. Its infrastructure currently supports more than 100,000 mining machines and serves a community of over 9 million users. The company utilizes mining rigs from leading manufacturers and continuously invests in AI-integrated optimization to enhance performance and cost efficiency.

ZA Miner is also registered in the UK and adheres to financial and environmental compliance standards, ensuring transparency and operational integrity.

The Passive Income Platform for 2025 and Beyond

With over 10 million users and 100,000+ mining machines, ZA Miner is rapidly emerging as the go-to cloud mining provider in 2025. Investors are choosing ZA Miner not just for its returns, but for its transparency, performance, and environmental leadership.

Key benefits include:

  • Regulatory Assurance – UK-registered and compliant

Accessible Contracts, Consistent Payouts

The platform offers a range of flexible contracts beginning at $100, with daily rewards and principal returns structured for predictability. Features include:

Positioning for Broader Participation in 2025

As crypto adoption continues to diversify, ZA Miner is positioning its infrastructure and services to meet the growing interest from outside the typical blockchain community. The company expects demand to rise steadily through the second half of the year as individual investors seek out stable, tech-driven income opportunities.

ZA FUNDINGS LTD (ZA Miner), established in 2020 and based in the UK, delivers advanced cloud mining services backed by renewable energy and enterprise-grade security. The company’s goal is to build an inclusive, sustainable mining network that empowers global users to participate in digital asset ecosystems efficiently and responsibly.

data.title: Keep Fannie and Freddie on tight financial leashes
data.description:
President Trump hinted anew about reprivatizing the US mortgage buyers while suggesting an implicit federal backstop would be explicit. A $160 bln capital shortfall needs attention first. And if taxpayers are on the hook for losses, then they deserve a big stake paying dividends.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Gabriel Rubin on Bluesky and LinkedIn.

CONTEXT NEWS

President Donald Trump said on May 27 that the U.S. government would retain its mortgage guarantees and oversight role for federally sponsored mortgage enterprises Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac if they are spun out of conservatorship.

CASE DETAILS: The filed complaint alleges that defendants made false statements and/or concealed that: (i) Avis Budget crafted and implemented a plan to significantly accelerate its fleet rotation in the fourth quarter of 2024; (ii) the foregoing acceleration shortened the useful life of the majority of the Company's vehicles in the Americas segment, thereby reducing their recoverable value; (iii) as a result, Avis Budget would be forced to recognize billions of dollars in impairment charges and incur substantial losses; (iv) all the foregoing was likely to, and did, have a significant negative impact on the Company's financial results; (v) accordingly, Avis Budget's financial and/or business prospects were overstated; and (vi) as a result, defendants' public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.

WHAT'S NEXT? If you suffered a loss in Avis Budget stock during the relevant time frame - even if you still hold your shares - go to https://zlk.com/pslra-1/avis-budget-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=151634&wire=1&utm_campaign=11 to learn about your rights to seek a recovery. There is no cost or obligation to participate.

data.title: Canada's Q1 Productivity Tops Expectations, But Real Story Is Slow Compensation Growth, Says Rosenberg Research
data.description:
Canada's Q1 labor productivity grew at 0.2% quarter over quarter in Q1, ahead of expectations of a 0.1% growth, said Rosenberg Research after Wednesday's release of the data.

Seasonally adjusted, business productivity rose 0.2% quarter over quarter and the year-over-year pace is 0.9%, the fastest since Q4 2020. However, Rosenberg Research sees clear evidence of a split in the economy, which Rosenberg thinks is driven by some pre-tariff distortions.

Goods sector productivity rose 0.8% quarter over quarter, with manufacturing productivity actually rising 0.2% after four straight quarterly declines. Service-sector productivity fell 0.5% quarter over quarter, in part because it's less vulnerable to pre-tariff distortions.

Last week, Canada's Q1 gross domestic product growth came in above expectations at 2.2% annualized — but Rosenberg assessed that this was partly due to both strong export growth and inventory build-up ahead of possible United States tariffs. The productivity numbers are likely suffering from a similar distortion, with strong pre-tariff demand artificially boosting factory utilization and, so, productivity.

This will revert in Q2, as much of the defensive exporting and inventory buildup has already happened, it stated.

Nominal compensation rose just 0.1% quarter over quarter, a clear sign of the disinflationary wage trend that Rosenberg has been seeing, and perhaps the most important data point in this print. That is the slowest wage growth since Q3 2022 and is a very soft number.

With compensation in check, unit labor costs rose 0.1% quarter over quarter, the slowest pace since Q3 2020 and a welcome sign of normalization that will help support Canada's competitiveness. In US dollar terms, unit labor costs fell 2.4%, after a 2.2% drop in Q4 2024.

Productivity in real estate and leasing services fell a "stunning" 3.9%, the biggest drop since Q2 2022 and a sign of deep distress in Canada's real estate sector, which is highly rate-sensitive. The sector picture was reasonably broad, with only eight of sixteen sectors showing productivity declines, but services looked worse than goods overall.

The productivity and GDP numbers come ahead of the Bank of Canada policy meeting on Wednesday. Investors are seeing a modest rally in the Canadian dollar (CAD or loonie) so far this year, mostly because of the US dollar (USD) weakness but also because the BoC seems behind the curve.

Rosenberg doesn't expect this productivity data to change the negative trendline that the Canadian economy faces, and it sees more BoC easing ahead as Q2 reverts to a more typical pattern.

data.title: Ryman Hospitality Closes $625 Million Senior Notes Offering
data.description:
Ryman Hospitality Properties said Wednesday that its units, RHP Hotel Properties and RHP Finance, closed a $625 million private placement of 6.5% senior notes due 2033.

Net proceeds from the offering will be used to fund a portion of the $865 million acquisition of the JW Marriott Phoenix Desert Ridge Resort and Spa located in Phoenix, Arizona, according to the company.

The balance of the purchase price will be funded with proceeds from an underwritten registered offering of shares.

The company said it will redeem the notes if the Desert Ridge acquisition is not consummated.

data.title: Hoist Finance Closes Oversubscribed SEK1 Billion Bond Offering
data.description:
Hoist Finance issued 1 billion kronor of senior preferred bonds, with the offering upsized and 3x oversubscribed.

Proceeds from the offering, initially with a volume of 500 million kronor, were earmarked by the asset manager for general corporate needs, according to a Wednesday release.

The bonds were priced at three-month Stockholm Interbank Offered Rate plus 215 basis points and will be listed on Euronext Dublin.
